title: backup.sh — backs up important directories to a timestamped archive
body: set -euo pipefail

title: ── config ────────────────────────────────────────────────

title: ── setup ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

body: log "cleaning old backups..." ls -t "$BACKUPDIR"/backup.tar.gz | tail -n +8 | while read -r old; do rm "$old" log "deleted old backup: $basename $old" done log "done." echo "backup saved: $ARCHIVE $SI

title: How to Build a Project
body: from raw idea to deployed product. the complete mental model. ---

title: the mindset shift
body: most beginners ask "how do I code this?" a senior developer asks "should I build this at all, and if yes, what is the simplest version that works?" senior developers are not smarter. they have seen mo

title: phase 1: understand the problem
body: before touching a keyboard, you must deeply understand what you are building and why.

title: ask these questions
body: what problem does this actually solve? be brutally honest. "I want to build a social network" is not a problem statement. "students at my college have no way to find study partners for specific subjec

title: write it down
body: write a one-paragraph description of the product: Product name helps specific user to solve specific problem by core mechanism. unlike current alternative, we key difference. example: StudyMatch helps

title: MVP first
body: MVP Minimum Viable Product is the smallest version that: - actually solves the core problem - is usable by real users - can validate whether your idea is right not the version with every feature you i

title: the feature list exercise
body: write down every feature you want. every single one. then: must have MVP — without this, the product does not work at all should have — makes the product significantly better, build after MVP nice to

title: prioritize ruthlessly
body: every feature has a cost: time to build, complexity to maintain, surface area for bugs. ask for every feature: "if we never build this, does the product still work?" if yes, it is not MVP. ---

title: phase 3: system design and architecture
body: now you think about how to build it, not what to build.

title: draw it on paper first
body: before any code, draw the high-level architecture: Mobile App / Web Browser | | HTTP requests | API Server | ┌────┴────┐ |         | Database Cache or more complex: React Frontend  Android App \

title: choose your architecture
body: monolith — one application handles everything. right choice for almost all beginners and small teams. one codebase → one database → one server microservices — separate services for separate features.

title: define the data flow
body: pick one core user action and trace it completely: example — user sends a message in chat app: 1. user types message, hits send 2. app validates: not empty, under 4000 chars 3. app encrypts message on

title: phase 4: database design
body: the database is the most important decision you make. it is the hardest to change later. think carefully.

title: identify your entities
body: entities are the things your app cares about. for a chat app: - users - messages - conversations - devices for push notifications for a student productivity app: - users - goals - daily logs - study s

title: design the schema
body: for each entity, define its fields and their types: users ├── id           uuid, primary key ├── username     text, unique, not null ├── email        text, unique, not null ├── password     text hashe

title: define relationships
body: one-to-many — one user has many messages. user is on the "one" side. use foreign key on the "many" side. many-to-many — users can be in many groups, groups have many users. use a junction table: users

title: normalization rule of thumb
body: do not store the same data in two places. if user's name changes, you should update it in ONE place and it reflects everywhere.

title: choose SQL or NoSQL
body: use SQL PostgreSQL when: - data has clear structure and relationships - you need transactions financial data, bookings - you need complex queries and joins - data integrity is critical use NoSQL Mongo

title: phase 5: API design
body: define your API before building it. this is the contract between frontend and backend.

title: REST conventions
body: GET    /api/users              list all users GET    /api/users/:id          get specific user POST   /api/users              create user PUT    /api/users/:id          replace user PATCH  /api/users/

title: define each endpoint
body: for every endpoint, define: - method and path - request body for POST/PUT/PATCH - response shape - possible errors example: POST /api/auth/login request body: { "email": "a@b.com", "password": "secret

title: phase 6: tech stack selection
body: choose boring, proven technology. the goal is to ship, not to use the newest thing.

title: questions to ask
body: do I already know this? using familiar tech is 10x faster than learning while building. does it have good documentation and community? you will get stuck. you need to find answers. is it appropriate f

body: web frontend:  React + Tailwind CSS + Vite mobile:        Kotlin + Jetpack Compose Android backend:       FastAPI Python or Express Node.js database:      PostgreSQL Supabase for free managed cache:

title: the golden rule
body: use the stack that gets you to a working product fastest. optimize later. ---

body: now you actually start. setup once, correctly, and you save hours later.

body: ~/warbird/ ├── .github/ │   └── workflows/ │       └── deploy.yml       ← github actions auto deploy ├── content/ │   ├── blog/                ← all blog posts go here │   │   └── first-flight.md │

title: 1. go to project
body: cd ~/warbird

title: 2. make your changes (write post, edit page etc)
3. preview locally
body: hugo server

title: 4. when happy, push to live
body: git add . git commit -m "what you did" git push

title: site goes live in ~2 minutes automatically
writing blog posts
body: every post is a markdown file inside content/blog/ create a new post: nano ~/warbird/content/blog/your-post-name.md required front matter at the top: --- title: "Your Post Title" date: 2026-03-04 summ

title: why vim
body: started using vim because i wanted full keyboard control...

title: keybinds i use daily
body: - dd to delete a line - yy to copy a line - p to paste

title: conclusion
body: vim is hard at first but worth it. push it live: git add . git commit -m "post: learning vim" git push naming convention for post files: good: learning-vim.md good: my-cp-journey.md bad:  My Post.md b

title: managing projects
body: edit the projects page: nano ~/warbird/content/projects/index.md format for each project:

title: project name
body: what it does, one or two lines visit →https://link-to-project.com

title: adding social links
body: STEP 1 — add to hugo.toml: nano ~/warbird/hugo.toml add your links in the params section: params description = "thoughts, projects and notes" author = "Abhishek Kumar" github = "https://github.com/abh

title: customizing the look
body: all styles are in one file: nano ~/warbird/themes/warbird/static/css/main.css color variables at the top — change these to retheme entire site: :root { --bg:        1a1a14;   / main background / --bg2

title: custom domain setup
body: when you get a domain free .tech from github student pack etc: STEP 1 — update hugo.toml: nano ~/warbird/hugo.toml baseURL = "https://yourdomain.tech/" STEP 2 — add CNAME file: echo "yourdomain.tech"

title: troubleshooting
body: site not updating after push: - check Actions tab on github — is workflow green? - if red, check the error in the failed step - wait 2-3 mins after green before checking live site local preview broken

title: quick reference
body: | task | command | |---|---| | new blog post | nano content/blog/name.md | | preview locally | hugo server | | push live | git add . && git commit -m "" && git push | | edit styles | nano themes/warbi
